“How many calories should I eat?” is one of the most searched health questions in the world — and for good reason. Whether your goal is losing weight, gaining muscle, or simply maintaining a healthy body, everything starts with knowing your daily calorie number.
The problem? The math involves your age, gender, height, weight, activity level, and goals — and getting it wrong means you either stall your progress or push too hard. That’s where a good calorie calculator does the heavy lifting for you.
In this guide, you’ll learn exactly how many calories you need per day, how the science (BMR and TDEE) actually works, and how to adjust your intake for your goal. And if you’d rather skip the formulas, ToolSnak’s Free Calorie Calculator gives you a personalized number in seconds.
Try it now → Free Calorie Calculator

What Is a Calorie (And Why Does It Matter)?
A calorie is simply a unit of energy. The food you eat provides calories; your body burns calories to stay alive and move. The balance between the two — calories in vs. calories out — is what determines whether you lose, gain, or maintain weight.

• Eat fewer calories than you burn → you lose weight

• Eat more than you burn → you gain weight

• Eat the same → you maintain
The whole game is knowing your personal “burn” number. That’s what the calculator finds.

BMR vs. TDEE: The Two Numbers That Matter
To understand your calorie needs, you need to know two things:
BMR — Basal Metabolic Rate
Your BMR is the number of calories your body burns at complete rest — just to keep you alive. Breathing, circulating blood, cell repair, maintaining temperature. Even if you stayed in bed all day, you’d still burn your BMR.
TDEE — Total Daily Energy Expenditure
Your TDEE is your BMR plus all the movement you do — walking, working, exercising, even digesting food. This is your real daily calorie need, and the number you should eat against.
Key rule: Eat against your TDEE, never your BMR. BMR is your survival minimum; TDEE is your actual daily burn.

The Formula: How Calorie Needs Are Calculated
Most modern calculators (including ToolSnak’s) use the Mifflin-St Jeor Equation, widely considered the most accurate BMR formula today.
For men:
BMR = (10 × weight in kg) + (6.25 × height in cm) − (5 × age) + 5For women:
BMR = (10 × weight in kg) + (6.25 × height in cm) − (5 × age) − 161Then multiply BMR by your activity factor to get TDEE:
Activity LevelMultiplierDescriptionSedentary× 1.2Little or no exercise, desk jobLightly active× 1.375Light exercise 1–3 days/weekModerately active× 1.55Moderate exercise 3–5 days/weekVery active× 1.725Hard exercise 6–7 days/weekExtra active× 1.9Physical job or 2× daily training
Worked Example
A 30-year-old man, 80 kg, 180 cm, trains 4 days a week:

• BMR = (10 × 80) + (6.25 × 180) − (5 × 30) + 5 = 1,780 kcal/day

• TDEE = 1,780 × 1.55 = 2,759 kcal/day (maintenance)
To lose ~0.5 kg/week, he’d eat about 2,259 kcal. To gain lean muscle, about 3,009 kcal.
ToolSnak’s calculator runs all of this automatically — you just enter your details.

How to Adjust Calories for Your Goal
Once you know your TDEE (maintenance), you adjust from there:
GoalAdjustmentResultWeight loss−250 to −500 kcal/day~0.5 kg (1 lb) loss per weekMaintain weightEat at TDEEStable weightMuscle gain+200 to +300 kcal/dayLean gain with training
Important: 1 kg of body fat ≈ 7,700 calories (1 lb ≈ 3,500). So a 500-calorie daily deficit theoretically loses about half a kilo a week.
⚠️ Don’t overdo it. Cutting more than 1,000 calories a day or losing more than 2 lbs/week can slow your metabolism, cause muscle loss, and backfire. Slow and steady wins.

How to Use ToolSnak’s Calorie Calculator
The tool is built to be simple and personalized:

• Open the Calorie Calculator

• Enter your Age, Gender, Height, and Weight

• Select your Activity Level (be honest — most people overestimate!)

• Choose your Goal — lose, maintain, or gain

You can even pick your output language — handy for non-English speakers.

What Affects Your Calorie Needs?

• Age — calorie needs decrease as you get older (BMR drops ~2% per decade after 30)

• Gender — men generally need more calories due to higher muscle mass

• Weight & height — larger bodies burn more calories

• Activity level — the biggest variable you control

• Muscle mass — muscle burns more calories than fat, even at rest

Common Calorie-Counting Mistakes to Avoid

• Overestimating activity level — about 80% of people rank themselves one tier too high. When in doubt, pick the lower level.

• Confusing BMR with TDEE — always eat against TDEE.

• Under-reporting food — studies show people underestimate intake by 20–40% (snacks, oils, and restaurant meals add up fast).

• Panicking over daily weight swings — water and sodium can move the scale 1–2 kg overnight. Use a 7-day average.

• Cutting too aggressively — extreme deficits cost you muscle and slow your metabolism.

Frequently Asked Questions
How many calories should I eat to lose weight? Take your TDEE and subtract 250–500 calories per day for steady, sustainable fat loss of about 0.5 kg (1 lb) per week.
How many calories should I eat to gain muscle? Add 200–300 calories above your TDEE and pair it with strength training and adequate protein.
Which formula does the calculator use? It’s based on the Mifflin-St Jeor equation, the most accurate BMR formula for the general population.
Is 1,200 calories a day safe? Very low intakes aren’t right for everyone and can be unhealthy. Always calculate your personal number and consult a doctor or registered dietitian before any aggressive plan.
How accurate is a calorie calculator? It gives a strong, science-based estimate. Individual metabolism varies, so eat at your target for 2–3 weeks, track results, and adjust by ±100 calories if needed.
Do men and women need different calories? Yes — the formula adjusts for gender (men add 5, women subtract 161 in Mifflin-St Jeor), and men typically need more due to greater muscle mass.

A Quick Health Note
This calculator gives science-based estimates to guide your nutrition — but it isn’t medical advice. Calorie needs vary with genetics, hormones, medical conditions, pregnancy, and more. For a personalized plan, especially if you have health conditions or want to lose significant weight, consult a doctor or registered dietitian.
